title: Bonjour ! — Greetings & introducing yourself
objective: Greet people, say your name, and use basic courtesy words (A1: can use familiar everyday expressions and introduce oneself).

bridge.title: Bridge from Hindi / English: French has gender — and so do you
bridge.body: Every French noun is masculine or feminine: **un ami** (a male friend) vs **une amie** (a female friend). This feels strange in English, but Hindi already does it — किताब (kitaab, 'book') is feminine, घर (ghar, 'house') is masculine, and adjectives change to agree. Use that instinct as a bridge. ⚠️ Caution: the gender of a word in Hindi does NOT predict its gender in French — treat Hindi gender as an *awareness* tool, not a rule. Always learn each French noun together with its un/une.

example.french: Bonjour ! Priya salue Arjun à Mumbai.
example.englishGloss: Hello! Priya greets Arjun in Mumbai.
example.hindiGloss: नमस्ते! प्रिया मुंबई में अर्जुन का अभिवादन करती है।

example.french: Au revoir ! À demain à Jaipur.
example.englishGloss: Goodbye! See you tomorrow in Jaipur.

example.french: Arjun est un ami de Bangalore.
example.englishGloss: Arjun is a friend from Bangalore.

example.french: Priya est une amie de Delhi.
example.englishGloss: Priya is a friend from Delhi.

exercises.prompt: Priya meets her teacher in the morning. Which word does she use to greet him politely?
exercises.explanation: « Bonjour » is the standard polite greeting. « Salut » is informal; « Merci » means thank you; « Au revoir » means goodbye.

exercises.prompt: What does « Je m'appelle Arjun » mean?
exercises.explanation: « Je m'appelle … » literally means 'I call myself …', i.e. 'My name is …'.

exercises.text: At the end of the call, Priya says: « {{}} ! À demain. »
Priya is a female friend, so in French: Priya est {{}} amie.
exercises.hint: Feminine indefinite article (like the gender of किताब).

exercises.prompt: Match each French word to its English meaning.
Build the sentence: “My name is Priya.”
